retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Clinical Significance -- D-Dimer for Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Under normal physiological conditions, hemostasis ensures the controlled formation of a clot, followed by its timely breakdown. Both coagulation and fibrinolysis become excessively activated in disseminated intravascular coagulation, resulting in ongoing clot formation and subsequent clot breakdown. Common triggers for disseminated intravascular coagulation include sepsis, trauma, malignancy, obstetric complications, and hemolysis. [29] [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Coagulopathies -- Clinical Significance -- Increased D-dimers
retrieved_docs.content: Venous thromboembolism: Incorporated in pretest probability clinical scores Disseminated intravascular coagulation Acute promyelocytic leukemia Other: Pregnancy, cancer, hospitalization, sepsis, and surgery [81] [139]

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Clinical Significance -- D-Dimer for Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians can expect elevated D-dimer levels in both acute and chronic disseminated intravascular coagulation. Other laboratory findings may include low or normal fibrinogen levels, thrombocytopenia, and either normal or prolonged prothrombin time, international normalized ratio, and partial thromboplastin time (aPTT). These values are also helpful in monitoring treatment response. In chronic disseminated intravascular coagulation, clotting times may remain within normal limits, and thrombocytopenia may be mild or even absent.

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Clinical Significance -- D-Dimer for Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Acute disseminated intravascular coagulation can lead to severe bleeding due to the rapid consumption of clotting factors in response to widespread exposure of blood to tissue factors and subsequent thrombin generation. Elevated fibrin degradation products further impair hemostasis by disrupting fibrin polymerization and inhibiting platelet binding to fibrinogen, undermining stable clot formation and platelet aggregation.

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Clinical Significance -- D-Dimer for Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Disseminated intravascular coagulation is both a clinical and laboratory diagnosis. Thrombocytopenia, consumption of coagulation factors, and fibrinolysis confirmed by a prolonged PT, aPTT, and elevated D-dimer confirm the diagnosis of acute disseminated intravascular coagulation. The presence of bleeding and thrombosis supports the diagnosis but is not required. Clinicians consider the diagnosis of chronic disseminated intravascular coagulation in patients with evidence of fibrinolysis, such as an elevated D-dimer, particularly in the context of underlying conditions, such as advanced malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Clinical Significance -- D-Dimer for Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic disseminated intravascular coagulation develops when the bloodstream is exposed to lower levels of tissue factors either continuously or intermittently over time. Patients with chronic disseminated intravascular coagulation also have excess consumption of coagulation factors and platelets. However, their production typically keeps pace with demand. The liver also effectively clears fibrin degradation products. As a result, patients with chronic disseminated intravascular coagulation are more prone to thrombotic events rather than bleeding complications.

retrieved_docs.title: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: No single history, physical exam, or laboratory component can lead to a diagnosis of or rule out DIC; therefore, a combination of subjective, objective, and laboratory findings should be utilized. Laboratory findings suggestive of DIC include an increased prothrombin time (PT) and an increased partial thromboplastin time, as well as a decreased fibrinogen level as widespread activation and consumption of the clotting cascade occurs. The overall platelet count and hematocrit level may be reduced as well. Schistocytes or fragmented erythrocytes are also commonly seen on a peripheral blood smear. The presence of fibrin split products additionally has a high sensitivity but low specificity for DIC. A specific scoring system to assess the presence of DIC was established in 2007. This score includes platelet count, fibrin markers such as D-dimer, PT, and fibrinogen level, with a score over 5 indicating a high likelihood for overt DIC. [18] [19] [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Potential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: As discussed previously, a D-dimer can aid in excluding the diagnosis of deep vein thrombosis or pulmonary embolism. Additionally, a D-dimer helps establish the diagnosis of disseminated intravascular coagulation, evaluates patients with a venomous snake bite for consumptive coagulopathy, and assesses patients with cryptogenic stroke for the risk of underlying malignancy. However, clinicians must remember that, due to its lack of specificity, an elevated D-dimer test result alone does not confirm the presence of any particular illness; instead, they should use D-dimer results in combination with other diagnostic parameters.

retrieved_docs.title: Hemolytic Uremic Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis -- Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation (DIC)
retrieved_docs.content: DIC is the systemic activation of the coagulation cascade and is characterized by abnormal coagulation studies, including prolonged prothrombin time and activated partial thromboplastin time, elevated D dimer, and elevated fibrin degradation products, which are usually normal in HUS. Patients with DIC usually have serious underlying illnesses such as septic shock, trauma, or malignancy.

retrieved_docs.title: Rhabdomyolysis -- Pathophysiology -- Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
retrieved_docs.content: The heme protein has the potential to cause many inflammatory actions; it activates platelets, functions as a damage-associated molecular pattern, induces apoptosis, damages mitochondria, activates inflammasomes, and produces cytokines. Heme is also directly thrombogenic by promoting the formation of neutrophil extracellular traps and inducing platelet degranulation, macrophage activation, and tissue factor expression. [42] In an underlying inflammatory and thrombogenic state, disseminated intravascular coagulation may also be due to thromboplastin released during muscle injury. [45] [46] Laboratory values will show increased prothrombin time, activated thromboplastin time, international normalized ratio, serum D-dimer levels, and decreased platelet count and fibrinogen levels.

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The D-dimer test has high sensitivity but low specificity, resulting in a high negative predictive value, which makes it helpful for excluding diagnoses such as deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, and disseminated intravascular coagulation in the appropriate clinical setting. Clinicians must take into account that pregnancy, trauma, malignancy, surgery, and liver disease can all cause elevations of the D-dimer.

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The following is a list of indications for measuring a D-dimer: Exclusion of deep vein thrombosis in patients who are low or intermediate risk Exclusion of pulmonary embolism in patients who are low or intermediate risk Diagnosis and monitoring of disseminated intravascular coagulation Monitoring of snake venom poisoning Evaluation of patients with cryptogenic stroke for the risk of possible underlying malignancy Prognostic assessment of patients who undergo recanalization after a stroke Prognostic assessment of patients with COVID-19 [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: D-Dimer Test -- Clinical Significance -- D-Dimer and Snake Bites
retrieved_docs.content: An additional potential use of the D-dimer test is in assessing venom-induced consumption coagulopathy after a snake bite. Patients who experience a bite from a venomous snake and experience collapse, seizures, weakness, paralysis, respiratory failure, shock, coagulopathy, or acute kidney injury should receive antivenom. In many cases, an increase in D-dimer is the first indicator of coagulopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Coagulation Pathways -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: COVID best exemplifies the initial onset of severe pneumonia or acute respiratory distress syndrome, followed closely by a terminal coagulopathic phase known as disseminated intravascular coagulation. [18] [19] The viral infection triggers a massive inflammatory response, often referred to as a cytokine storm. Interleukin-6 is released within the storm, concurrent with other infectious and tissue injury, and consequently causes a decrease in protein S, a hypergammaglobulinemia, and disseminated intravascular coagulation. With the fibrin and platelet deposition of progressive disseminated intravascular coagulation, there is an increased risk of hemorrhage, as the factors and thrombocytes themselves are consumed faster than they are replaced. Similarly, the fibrin is cross-linked by factor XIII, and as the strands are broken, these adhered sections become the characteristic D-dimers. The increase in D-dimer concentration has been advocated as a means to monitor the disease severity and mortality risk; however, this opinion is debated. Although many diseases with disseminated intravascular coagulation display vascular thrombi, in COVID, the presence of micro- and macrovascular thrombi is accentuated. Autopsy findings reveal not only interstitial damage and edema but also significant pulmonary thrombosis. In COVID, there are additional clotting issues in the other organs, with an overall multi-organ failure. With such widespread thromboses, many patients can succumb to the hemorrhage of a consumptive coagulopathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Assess the clinical manifestations of disseminated intravascular coagulation. Implement management considerations for disseminated intravascular coagulation. Explain the importance of utilizing an interprofessional team to manage patients with disseminated intravascular coagulation. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Interpretation of Blood Clotting Studies and Values (PT, PTT, aPTT, INR, Anti-Factor Xa, D-Dimer) -- Clinical Significance -- D-Dimer
retrieved_docs.content: Disseminated intravascular coagulation: DIC is a serious condition where the blood clotting process is activated throughout the body, leading to widespread clot formation and consumption of clotting factors. This can result in bleeding complications. Elevated D-dimer levels are often seen in DIC, and D-dimer testing may be used to help diagnose and monitor this condition. Stroke and arterial thrombosis: While D-dimer is more commonly associated with venous thrombosis, elevated levels may also be observed in certain arterial thrombotic events, such as stroke. D-dimer testing is not as specific for arterial events as it is for venous events, so its utility in assessing arterial thrombosis is limited.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2737
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 75, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease); using dextran 75 in this condition may promote clot formation and worsen clinical outcomes, so clinicians should avoid prescribing it to individuals who have thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1996
retrieved_docs.content: Thrombolytic therapeutic role is identified as a specific type of therapeutic role, indicating that the use of thrombolytic agents to dissolve blood clots constitutes a particular therapeutic approach within the broader category of therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 252
retrieved_docs.content: is a subclass of blood‑forming organ drugs and encompasses the more specific group “vitamin K and other hemostatics,” which includes agents that promote clot formation through vitamin K‑dependent pathways. Together, these interrelated classifications illustrate how antithrombotic, hemostatic, and enzymatic agents are organized within the pharmacological landscape of blood and blood‑forming organ therapeutics, providing a structured framework for understanding their mechanisms and clinical applications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1696
retrieved_docs.content: Deoxyribonucleases / Plasmin functions as the active ingredient of human fibrinolysin, a therapeutic agent that promotes fibrinolysis by breaking down fibrin clots. By incorporating the enzymatic activities of both deoxyribonucleases and plasmin, this formulation leverages plasmin’s natural ability to cleave fibrin and deoxyribonucleases’ capacity to degrade nucleic acids, enhancing the overall efficacy of fibrinolysin in clinical settings where clot dissolution is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1751
retrieved_docs.content: Factor VIII may be used to treat coagulation and hemorrhagic disorders, as it helps correct deficiencies in the clotting cascade that underlie these bleeding conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 4 -- Community 2852
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 40, a low‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in the presence of الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 40 can increase blood viscosity and promote clot formation, its use in patients with thrombotic disorders may exacerbate the underlying condition and raise the risk of further vascular occlusion. Consequently, clinicians should avoid administering Dextran 40 to individuals diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this risk.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 163
retrieved_docs.content: vessels and general fluoroscopy‑guided thrombolysis; percutaneous thrombolysis of the axillary vein and of central venous catheters; and computed tomography of the thorax with contrast, providing anatomical visualization and accurate catheter placement. Enzymatic agents such as deoxyribonucleases/Plasmin (derived from plasmin and containing human fibrinolysin) and urokinase‑type activators convert plasminogen to active plasmin, reinforcing the fibrinolytic pathway. Collectively, fibrinolytic enzymes, LMWHs, direct Factor Xa inhibitors, synthetic antithrombotic compounds, heparin‑derived anticoagulants and contrast media form a coordinated network that enables anticoagulation, targeted thrombolysis, and precise imaging guidance, thereby enhancing the prevention and treatment of thrombotic disorders and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1950
retrieved_docs.content: Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are plasma‑expanding agents that can help prevent venous thrombosis (التخثر الوريدي). However, both Dextran 70 and Dextran 75 are contraindicated in patients who have thrombotic disease (الاعتلال الخثري). This dual relationship underscores the importance of evaluating a patient’s underlying thrombotic condition before administering either Dextran 70 or Dextran 75, as their antithrombotic benefit in the venous system must be balanced against the risk of worsening an existing thrombotic disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 1339
retrieved_docs.content: BLOOD AND BLOOD FORMING ORGAN DRUGS constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes antithrombotic agents; antithrombotic agents are a specific subclass of these drugs and are used to inhibit platelet aggregation and coagulation, thereby preventing the formation of pathological blood clots in various clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1571
retrieved_docs.content: Antifibrinolytics are agents that inhibit the breakdown of fibrin clots and are biochemically linked to vitamin K compounds, encompassing both vitamin K1 and vitamin K2. Alfa‑2‑antiplasmin is a specific antifibrinolytic protein that functions within this class, contributing to the stabilization of formed clots. The association of antifibrinolytics with both forms of vitamin K underscores their role in the vitamin K–dependent activation of clotting factors, which is essential for normal hemostasis. Recognizing that antifibrinolytic activity can arise from or interact with both vitamin K1 and vitamin K2 informs clinical decisions in managing bleeding disorders, anticoagulant therapy, and related coagulation abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K and other hemostatics produce hemostatické účinky, meaning they exert the physiological hemostatic effects that stop bleeding by promoting clot formation and maintaining vascular integrity. These hemostatic actions are central to the therapeutic role of vitamin K and related agents in controlling hemorrhage and supporting normal coagulation processes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 378
retrieved_docs.content: Heparin group antithrombotic drugs constitute a class of anticoagulant medications that achieve their therapeutic effect primarily through interaction with antithrombin III, a plasma protein that inhibits clotting factors. Within this drug class, dalteparin and nadroparin are specific low‑molecular‑weight heparin agents that share the same mechanism of enhancing antithrombin III activity to prevent thrombus formation. Recognizing dalteparin and nadroparin as members of the heparin group clarifies their pharmacologic relationship and guides clinicians in selecting appropriate antithrombotic therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 225
retrieved_docs.content: and reagents, are essential for visualizing vascular structures in these procedures and in related imaging studies such as contrast‑enhanced computed tomography of the thorax. Together, heparin‑based anticoagulants prevent new clot formation, antithrombin III amplifies their effect, fibrinolytic enzymes actively dissolve existing thrombi, and regulatory proteins fine‑tune the coagulation cascade, while contrast‑enhanced imaging provides precise localization and monitoring, forming an integrated diagnostic‑therapeutic framework that optimizes patient care in thrombotic disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 0 -- Community 16
retrieved_docs.content: anticholesteremic agents, hematologic substances, antithrombotic and fibrinolytic drugs, appropriate dosage forms, and contrast media are coordinated to manage vascular and coagulation disorders, perform urinary‑tract interventions, and support diagnostic imaging.

Conversely, fibrinolytika are explicitly excluded from endovascular intracranial prolonged administrations of non‑thrombolytic agents such as arterial catheter placement and diagnostic angiography, underscoring the need to match drug selection with therapeutic intent.
